sutta:
Monks, I know not of any other single thing so uncontrollable as the uncultivated mind the uncultivated mind is indeed a thing uncontrollable.
Monks, I know not of any other single thing so controllable as the cultivated mind the cultivated mind is indeed a controllable thing.
Monks, I know not of any other single thing so conducive to great loss as the uncultivated mind the uncultivated mind indeed conduces to create loss monks I know not of any other single thing so conducive to great profit as the cultivated mind the cultivated mind indeed conduces to great profit.
commentary:
I stopped here for a while to comment so if the mind is not cultivated now it is very difficult to control it in fact when we practice meditation especially samatha meditation we are exercising control over the mind every moment when the mind tries to run away we bring it back to one single object it runs away again because that is the tendency of the mind to run and keep on running and we bring it back to one object so every moment we are exercising control over the mind and if we keep on doing that then when samādhi arises we are able to control the mind samādhi is the result of controlling the mind so when we have samādhi then the mind is controlled already and that is why the Buddha says there is no other single thing so controllable as a cultivated mind."
    
    snippets:But it's not I forgot to mention when we cultivate the mind there are two aspects of it one is cultivation of the mind sometimes the word used is bhavana bhavana is to develop to cultivate the mind the development of the mind or cultivation of the mind is one aspect as I mentioned just now is samatha to train the mind to control the mind the other aspect is vipassana vipassana is to use that controlled mind to contemplate and to get wisdom so samatha and vipassana are both necessary now if our mind is uncultivated here the Buddha says it conduces to great loss why because if we have an uncultivated mind the hindrances are very strong sensual desire is very strong ill will sloth and torpor restlessness and worry and doubt and it's obvious if somebody is troubled by sensual desire he becomes very restless trying to satisfy it and a lot of his energy time and money are wasted or if he has a lot of ill will then he will not be successful nobody likes to deal with a person full of anger and people avoid such a person and because the mind is not clear and not cultivated one makes wrong decisions one cannot see things clearly and is like in a daze so that is why the Buddha says it conduces to great loss whereas a cultivated mind conduces to great profit a cultivated mind is a person with a lot of wisdom in another sutta the Buddha says what is the condition for great wisdom and insight the best condition is a mind that is bright and clear a mind that is bright and clear is a mind that has developed samādhi the one pointedness of mind because the word is jhāna jhāna literally means brightness or incandescence so when a person develops jhāna the mind becomes bright and that is the best condition for insight that is why it leads to great profit.
